narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 42% chance that you would not pass—higher than the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 39, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, family income in the lower-middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on those most predictive of bar success. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the 6th decile average among students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance later in your studies. Counterbalancing this, your LSAT score stood out positively as one of the top influences, being above the 37.27 average for bar passers and pulling the prediction toward success. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 also helped in that direction, though it fell short of the 3.24 average for those who passed, making it the third-most influential factor overall. While your full-time enrollment aligns with the vast majority of successful students and your family background fits within the broader mix of passers, these played smaller roles compared to your grades and test scores. In essence, the model saw your early promise in the LSAT and undergrad work overshadowed by a concerning drop-off in your Year 3 ranking, setting you apart from typical admitted students whose deciles trended higher across the board. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many students improve dramatically with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking tutoring to boost your later-year performance, perhaps exploring programs that offer bar prep support or grade enhancement opportunities. You might also gather stronger letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience or work ethic. With focused steps like these, you can reshape your profile for future applications and increase your odds of bar success.
